mainEntity.name: How much does a Jaguar XJ 3.2 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned XJ 3.2 engine typically costs £2,500–£5,000 supply only. Fitting adds £1,500–£2,500 depending on location and workshop rates. Customers have reported dealer quotes of around £15,000–£30,000 for engine replacement, while reconditioning or rebuilding is often in the £8,000–£10,000 range. A low-mileage replacement engine typically ranges from £3,000 to £6,000. Main dealer new engines are no longer available. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Jaguar XJ 3.2 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the AJ6 and AJ16 engines are among Jaguar's most durable straight-sixes. They are 'TUFF' and capable of 500,000 km (310,000 miles) with proper maintenance. The AJ6 was introduced in the XJ40 in 1992; the AJ16 followed in the X300 from 1994. Common issues include valve stem seals, upper timing chain tensioner wear, and O2 sensor failure — all manageable with proper maintenance.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Jaguar XJ 3.2?

acceptedAnswer.text: The main known issues are: (1) Valve stem seal failure — causing blue smoke on start-up, (2) Upper timing chain tensioner wear — causing rattling noise on cold or hot start, and (3) Oxygen sensor failure — causing black smoke, rough idle and poor fuel economy.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Jaguar XJ 3.2 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept £3,000–£8,000 XJ, a £3,500–£5,000 recon is 40–165% of the car's value. For a clean, low-mileage example with good body and interior, replacement is often worth it given the XJ's rising classic status. However, if the car has other issues (rust, electrical faults), evaluate total restoration costs before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Jaguar XJ 3.2 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The XJ 3.2 uses either the AJ6 (XJ40, 1992–1994) or AJ16 (X300, 1994–1997) engine. The AJ6 was introduced in 1992 replacing the 2.9L engine. The AJ16 is a 'much-modified version' with distributor-less ignition. The engine code is stamped on the cylinder block. Tell us your registration and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I replace the upper timing chain tensioner without removing the engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the upper tensioner can be replaced without removing the timing cover. It is 'removed from the outside, and simple in any language'. This is the single most important maintenance item on the AJ6/AJ16. At the first sign of rattling, book an inspection immediately. If the tensioner fails and the chain snaps, a full engine replacement is usually the only option.

eraMap.title: Jaguar XJ 3.2 — Which Engine Does Your Car Actually Have?

rows.eraNote: 3.2L DOHC 24v straight-six — 200–221 hp. Introduced 1992 replacing 2.9L. Valve stem seals and upper timing chain tensioner are key weak points
3.2L DOHC 24v straight-six — 208–219 hp. Revised AJ16 with distributor-less ignition and improved engine management

replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an XJ 3.2 engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). The engine is longitudinally mounted in a front-engine, rear-wheel-drive layout.

cta.label: → Compare XJ 3.2 engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Valve Stem Seal Failure
problems.symptoms: Blue smoke from exhaust on start-up after the engine has been at rest, oil consumption, fouled spark plugs
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not progress to engine replacement if addressed)

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection if blue smoke appears on startup

problems.recommendation: Valve stem seals are a "common leak point" on these engines. This is one of the most frequently reported issues on the AJ6/AJ16, allowing oil to dribble into the cylinders and causing blue smoke at start-up. If you notice blue smoke on first start-up after the car has been resting, have the valve stem seals inspected. Catching this early prevents fouled spark plugs and catalytic converter damage.

failureLink.label: Read the full valve stem seal guide → /failures/valve-stem-seals/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

problems.title: Upper Timing Chain Tensioner Wear
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold or hot start, especially at 1,200–1,400 rpm, that subsides as oil pressure builds
problems.typicalMileage: 100,000+ miles — tensioner can fail with age

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The upper timing chain tensioner is "well documented as failing with age" on the AJ16. There is NO adjustment for the AJ16 timing chain. The top tensioner in the cylinder head right-hand front corner can fail, and even "new update" versions can fail. At the first sign of rattling, get the tensioner inspected immediately. A £200–£500 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than a £3,500–£7,000 engine replacement. Replacing the upper tensioner is a straightforward job — the timing cover does not need to be removed.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain tensioner guide

problems.title: Oxygen Sensor Failure (Running Rich)
problems.symptoms: Black smoke during acceleration, spark plugs covered in soot, engine stalling at idle, excessive fuel consumption, motor oil smelling of gasoline
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (does not progress to engine replacement if addressed)
problems.recommendation: A common issue on the XJ 3.2 is running rich due to failed oxygen sensors. Symptoms include black smoke under acceleration, sooty spark plugs, rough idle, and fuel consumption dropping significantly — one owner reported consumption improving from 6 km/l to 10–11 km/l after replacing two broken O2 sensors. If you experience these symptoms, have the O2 sensors inspected. Catching this early prevents catalytic converter damage.

failureLink.label: Read the full oxygen sensor guide → /failures/oxygen-sensor-failure/ [NEW FAILURE PAGE NEEDED]

buyingChecks.title: Buying This XJ 3.2? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with regular oil changes using correct spec oil
* No rattling on cold start (tensioner health)
* No blue smoke on start-up (valve stem seal health)
* Evidence of recent O2 sensor replacement or good fuel economy
avoidIf: * Cold-start rattle from the front of the engine — tensioner warning
* Blue smoke on start-up — valve stem seal failure
* Black smoke or very poor fuel economy — O2 sensor issues
* Poor service history — these engines are tough but need proper maintenance

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Jaguar XJ 3.2's AJ6 and AJ16 engines are among Jaguar's most durable straight-sixes. These engines are "TUFF" and capable of 500,000 km (310,000 miles) with proper maintenance. The AJ6 was introduced in the XJ40 in 1992, replacing the 2.9L engine. The AJ16 was a "much-modified version" with distributor-less ignition. Common issues are manageable: valve stem seals, upper timing chain tensioner wear, and O2 sensor failure. Buy a well-maintained example with documented service history — these engines reward proper care with exceptional longevity.

cta.label: → Compare XJ 3.2 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Jaguar XJ 3.2 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling from the front of the engine (timing chain tensioner warning)
* Hot Start — Any rattling after the engine has been at rest? (tensioner oil pressure loss)
* Blue Smoke — Any blue smoke on first start-up after rest? (valve stem seals)
* Running Rich — Black smoke under acceleration, sooty spark plugs, poor fuel economy (O2 sensors)
* Service History — Including which engine (AJ6 or AJ16) and confirmed oil change intervals
* Oil Leaks — Look for oil residue around the engine — AJ6/AJ16 are generally tight but check anyway
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Look for O2 sensor or timing-related codes
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
